content:
No fluff — just prompts that actually save you hours

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Austin Distel on UnsplashMost people use AI like a search engine. They type a short question, get a short answer, and move on. Then they wonder why AI hasn’t actually changed how much time they spend working.
The truth is, AI can do a lot more than answer quick questions. It can write your listing descriptions, plan your job search, draft your client proposals, and handle tasks that would normally eat up your entire afternoon. The difference isn’t the tool. It’s the prompt.
A short, lazy prompt gets a short, lazy answer. A detailed prompt — one that tells AI exactly who to be, what to consider, and how to format the output — gets something you could actually use right away.
Here are 5 prompts like that. Each one is built for a different situation, so pick the one that matches what you’re dealing with right now.
1. For real estate agents — listings that create desire before the open home
Writing a fresh, exciting property description for every listing gets tiring fast, especially when the houses start to blur together.
You are an elite real estate copywriter who has sold over $500M in luxury properties. Your listings are known for creating emotional desire before buyers ever step through the door.
Task: Write a premium property listing for [PROPERTY TYPE, e.g. 4-bedroom waterfront villa] located in [LOCATION]. The property features: [LIST 5–8 KEY FEATURES, e.g. infinity pool, chef’s kitchen, private dock].
Requirements:
Headline (max 12 words): Must evoke lifestyle and aspiration — not just describe the property
Opening paragraph (60–80 words): Paint a vivid “day in the life” picture of living here. Use sensory language — light, sound, feeling. Never start with the square footage.
Feature highlights (4–6 bullet points): Each bullet = one feature + why it matters to the buyer’s lifestyle.
Neighbourhood narrative (40–60 words): Paint the surrounding area as an extension of the lifestyle promise
Closing call-to-action (20–30 words): Create urgency without being pushy
Tone: Aspirational, confident, editorial — like a feature in Architectural Digest, not a classifieds ad.
Fill in the brackets with your actual property details, and you get a listing that sounds like it was written by someone who’s sold hundreds of homes — not a template.
2. For e-commerce sellers — product pages that convert browsers into buyers
Most product descriptions just list features. The ones that actually sell connect a feature to a feeling.
You are a conversion rate specialist who writes product page copy that turns browsers into buyers by combining persuasive storytelling with the specific information shoppers need to feel confident clicking “Add to Cart.”
Task: Write complete product page copy for [PRODUCT NAME] in the [NICHE/INDUSTRY] space. Target customer: [DESCRIBE — demographics, pain points, what they’ve tried before, what they really want].
Deliver:
Short description (40–60 words): Hook + core benefit + who it’s for. Every word earns its place.
Long description (200–250 words): Structured as a story: the problem → the old solution’s failure → why this product is different → what life looks like after purchase.
Feature/benefit table: 5–6 rows — Feature on left, “What this means for you” on right
Customer FAQ section (5 questions): The actual questions that stop people from buying
Tone: Conversational, specific, confidence-building. Sounds like a trusted friend who happens to know this product inside-out.
This one small shift — benefits instead of just features — is usually the difference between a listing people scroll past and one they actually buy from.
3. For freelancers — cold emails that actually get replies
Most freelance outreach gets ignored because it sounds like every other cold email in the inbox.
You are a freelance prospecting specialist who has cracked the code on cold outreach — your clients report 20–35% response rates on cold emails because every email is genuinely personalised, concise, and leads with value.
Task: Write a cold email pitch for a [FREELANCE PROFESSION] reaching out to [TARGET COMPANY TYPE] about helping them with [SPECIFIC SERVICE].
Email requirements:
Opening (1–2 sentences): Start with something specific about THEM — a specific observation about their business, content, product, or recent news
The hook (2–3 sentences): Connect what you noticed to a problem or opportunity
The offer (1–2 sentences): What specific thing can you do for them? Be concrete.
Social proof (1 sentence): One specific result from a similar client
The ask (1 sentence): The smallest possible ask — a 15-minute call
Total length: Under 150 words
Tone: Confident peer, not applicant.
Clients aren’t looking for the longest pitch in their inbox. They’re looking for the one that proves you actually understood their business before you hit send.
4. For small business owners — turning one negative review into a trust signal
How a business responds to a bad review often tells potential customers more than the review itself.
You are a small business reputation management specialist who understands that how a business responds to negative reviews tells potential customers far more about the business than the review itself.
Task: Write a response for a legitimate complaint, where something genuinely went wrong.
Response formula: Acknowledge + Apologise + Act + Invite Full response template (80–100 words) Tone: Humble, accountable, solution-focused
Key principle: You’re not writing for the reviewer — you’re writing for the 100 people who will read this.
That last line is the part most business owners forget. A calm, accountable response isn’t really for the unhappy customer — it’s for everyone else deciding whether to trust you.
5. For job seekers — a cover letter opening that doesn’t sound like everyone else’s
Most cover letters start the same way, which means most cover letters get skimmed and forgotten the same way too.
You are a hiring manager turned career coach who has read thousands of cover letters and can immediately tell which ones were written by people who want any job and which were written by people who want THIS job — and the difference is almost entirely in the first paragraph.
Task: Write a complete, compelling cover letter for [CANDIDATE NAME] applying for the role of [JOB TITLE] at [COMPANY NAME].
Opening paragraph (50–70 words): Do NOT start with “I am writing to apply for…” — everyone does. Instead: start with a specific observation about the company’s work, a relevant accomplishment of theirs, or a genuine insight about the challenge this role is designed to solve. Then connect yourself to it.
Paragraph 2 — The evidence paragraph: One specific, quantified achievement that proves you can do what the role requires.
Paragraph 3 — Why this company: One genuine, specific reason this company — not just this role type.
Tone: Confident, specific, genuine. A great cover letter sounds like a smart person who has done their homework and knows exactly why they belong here.
That one instruction — don’t open with “I am writing to apply for” — quietly fixes what’s wrong with 90% of cover letters.
Why detailed prompts like this actually matter
None of these prompts are complicated to write. They just take a bit more thought than typing “write me a product description” and hoping for the best.
The pattern across all five is the same: give AI a role, give it context, tell it exactly how to structure the output, and tell it what to avoid. That’s really the whole trick. Once you build that habit, you stop getting generic AI output and start getting things you can actually send, post, or submit with barely any editing.
If prompts like these are useful to you, I put together a full pack of 100 — covering real estate, e-commerce, freelancing, small business, and job hunting in a lot more depth. You can check it out here: 100 AI Prompts That Do The Work For You
